coredata.dc:title: Copper nanocluster coupling europium as an off-to-on fluorescence probe for the determination of phosphate ion in water samples
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Haiyan
Chen, Zhaohui
Huang, Yuming
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports an “off-to-on” fluorescence (FL) probe for sensitively and selectively detecting phosphate ions (Pi's). Fabrication of the probe was based on the competition between Pi's and tannic acid-stabilized copper nanoclusters (TA-Cu NCs) for Eu3+ binding. The addition of Eu3+ ions to TA-Cu NCs triggered the aggregation of TA-Cu NCs, which quenched the FL of TA-Cu NCs. After Pi addition, the aggregated TA-Cu NCs solubilized into the aqueous solution to facilitate the Pi-triggered dispersion of TA-Cu NCs. This phenomenon was due to the stronger binding ability between Pi's and Eu3+ than that between TA and Eu3+, leading to FL recovery of Cu NCs. The degree of redispersion of TA-Cu NCs was directly related to Pi concentration. Thus, Pi concentration can be quantitatively determined by the change in FL of the TA-Cu NCs dispersion. Under the optimized conditions, the change in FL presented a linear relationship with Pi concentration from 0.07μmolL−1 to 80μmolL−1. The limit of detection for Pi was 9.6×10−3 μmolL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. For Pi determination in real samples, only 1mL water sample was needed. The proposed probe was highly sensitive, free from the interference of other common species in aqueous media, and particularly useful for the fast and simple diagnosis of water-eutrophication extent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Phosphate ions
Fluorescence probe
Lanthanide ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The possible mechanism of the fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The FL emission spectra of the TA-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of Eu3+ ions from 0.1–60μmolL−1, λ ex=360nm; inset: a Stern–Volmer plot. (B) FL decays of TA-Cu NCs under different conditions.

The FL spectra of the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system after adding Pi's (A) from 0 to 1.0μmolL−1 and (B) from 1.0 to 80μmolL−1. Inset: plots of the values of (I−I 0)/I 0 at 430nm versus the concentrations of Pi's. I 0 and I are the FL intensities of TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ without and with Pi's, respectively. The error bars represent one standard deviation for three measurements.

(A) Kinetics of reaction between 3μmolL−1 TA-Cu NCs and 60μmolL−1 Eu3+ without and with different concentrations of Pi; (B) FL responses of different concentrations of the TA-Cu NCs at 430nm in different systems: TA-Cu NCs (black square); TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ (red round); TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+-Pi (green triangle); the concentration of Eu3+: 100μmolL−1; (C) effect of Eu3+concentration on the FL responses of 3μmolL−1 TA–Cu NCs in different systems: TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ (red round); TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+-Pi (black square); (D) effects of solution pH on the FL responses of 3μmolL−1 TA-Cu NCs in different systems: TA-Cu NCs (black square); TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ (red round); TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+–Pi (green triangle), the concentration of Eu3+: 60μmolL−1. I and I 0 are the FL intensities of TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system in the presence and absence of 10μmolL−1 Pi's, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Relative FL intensities of TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system at 430nm with addition of 10μmolL−1 Pi's and various cations (A) and anions (B) (10, 100, or 1000μmolL−1). I 0 and I are the FL intensities of TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system without and with various ions, respectively.
Schematic of fluorescent TA-Cu NCs for sensing Pi.
LOD comparison of this work with some established fluorescence methods for Pi.

row.entry: Cysteine capped CdS quantum dots and Ag NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of determining Pi's in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Copper nanocluster coupling europium as an off-to-on fluorescence probe for the determination of phosphate ion in water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Basic Department of Rongchang Campus, Southwest University, Chongqing 402460, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Basic Department of Rongchang Campus, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chongqing 400714, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports an “off-to-on” fluorescence (FL) probe for sensitively and selectively detecting phosphate ions (Pi's). Fabrication of the probe was based on the competition between Pi's and tannic acid-stabilized copper nanoclusters (TA-Cu NCs) for Eu3+ binding. The addition of Eu3+ ions to TA-Cu NCs triggered the aggregation of TA-Cu NCs, which quenched the FL of TA-Cu NCs. After Pi addition, the aggregated TA-Cu NCs solubilized into the aqueous solution to facilitate the Pi-triggered dispersion of TA-Cu NCs. This phenomenon was due to the stronger binding ability between Pi's and Eu3+ than that between TA and Eu3+, leading to FL recovery of Cu NCs. The degree of redispersion of TA-Cu NCs was directly related to Pi concentration. Thus, Pi concentration can be quantitatively determined by the change in FL of the TA-Cu NCs dispersion. Under the optimized conditions, the change in FL presented a linear relationship with Pi concentration from 0.07μmolL−1 to 80μmolL−1. The limit of detection for Pi was 9.6×10−3 μmolL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. For Pi determination in real samples, only 1mL water sample was needed. The proposed probe was highly sensitive, free from the interference of other common species in aqueous media, and particularly useful for the fast and simple diagnosis of water-eutrophication extent.

ce:section.ce:para: Excess phosphate ions (Pi's) in water can lead to increased algal growth, eutrophication, and reduced water quality. Thus, the development of sensitive, selective, and reliable analytical techniques for Pi determination is important [1,2]. Various methods, such as colorimetric [3], electrochemical [4,5] and fluorescence (FL) methods [6–9], have been proposed. Sensors based on changes induced by Pi's in FL are attractive because of their simplicity and low detection limit (LOD) [10,11]. For example, several FL approaches were reported for the detection of Pi's. Bai et al. proposed a photoluminescent sensor for Pi detection by using GQDs as an effective sensing platform with a detection limit of 1.0×10−1 μmolL−1 [6]. Wang et al. found that cysteine capped CdS quantum dots and Ag NPs could be used as FL probes for the detection of phosphate with a detection limit of 1.0×10−2 μmolL−1 [9]. Ganjali et al. reported a bis(8-hydroxy quinoline-5-solphonate) cerium(III) chloride as a fluorescent sensor for phosphate recognition with a detection limit of 2.5×10−2 μmolL−1 [12]. Mitra et al. proposed a FL quenching method for Pi using a glucose-based naphthylimino conjugate(L)–Zn2+ complex with a detection limit of 2.2μmolL−1 [13]. Hatai et al. reported that N,N-(ethoxy carbonyl methyl)-ethylenediamine–Cu2+ complex could be used as a FL chemosensor for inorganic phosphates with a detection limit of 1.0×10−1 μmolL−1 [14]. Zhao et al. proposed carbon dots-based FL probe for the detection of phosphate with a detection limit of 5.0×10−2 μmolL−1 [15]. However, fabricating an effective FL probe for Pi's is challenging because of the inherent tetrahedral structure of Pi's [16,17].

Recently, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have drawn considerable interest because of their unique electrical, physical, and optical properties for catalysis, biological imaging, and chemical sensors [18–20]. MNCs are generally composed of a few atoms, with a subnanometer-sized core, which provide the missing link between atomic and nanoparticle behavior in metals [21]. With sizes comparable to the Fermi wavelength of electrons, MNCs display molecule-like properties, including discrete electronic states and size-dependent FL [22,23]. Compared with quantum dots and organic dyes, MNCs possess excellent photostability, large Stokes shift, and low environmental hazard [24].

The FL of Au and Ag nanoclusters (NCs) have been extensively investigated because of their chemical stability and facile synthesis [19,24–26]. Compared with Au and Ag NCs, copper NCs (Cu NCs) are significantly cheaper and widely used in industries [27]. However, to date, only a few studies have applied Cu NCs as FL probes in biological and chemical sensing [28–31]. For example, Vilar-Vidal et al. proposed a selective and sensitive method for the detection of lead ions by using copper clusters as FL probes, which were electrochemically prepared. Interestingly, the as-prepared copper clusters exhibit efficient photocatalytic activity and can be used for the photocatalytic elimination of lead ions [32]. The observed effects can be explained in terms of an efficient electron transfer due to the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energy of the cluster overlapping with the ion redox potential, and a possible size dependence of the selectivity was also reported. Ling et al. synthesized stable and highly fluorescent polyethyleneimine capped Cu NCs for the label-free FL detection of Sudan dyes in food samples [33]. Gao et al. prepared bovine serum albumin-capped Cu NCs for the selective, sensitive and label-free FL detection of kojic acid in food stuff [34]. It is noted that in the previous works, macromolecular surface protecting ligands, such as DNA, proteins, and polymers, have been employed to synthesize Cu NCs. These templates have resulted in the formation of Cu NCs with large hydrodynamic radius, which limit the potential applications of Cu NCs. To overcome this limitation, by using small-molecule tannic acid as a stabilizing reagent, we have recently proposed a simple and facile strategy for the synthesis of FL Cu NCs for sensitive and selective sensing of ferric ions [35]. This method is based on the selective coordination of Fe3+ with tannic acid on the surface of TA-Cu NCs, leading to a strong FL quenching of the TA-Cu NCs via an electron transfer mechanism [35]. Here, we interestingly find that the lanthanide ions, including Eu3+, Yb3+, Tb3+ and Ce3+, also cause FL quenching of the TA-Cu NCs by acting as a bridge for the induction of Cu NC aggregation. With Eu3+ as an example, Scheme 1 shows that adding Eu3+ into the TA-Cu NCs triggers the aggregation of TA-Cu NCs, leading to the quenching of the FL of TA-Cu NCs. After adding Pi's, the aggregated TA-Cu NCs tend to solubilize into an aqueous solution to facilitate a dispersion of TA-Cu NCs triggered by Pi's (Scheme 1). This phenomenon is due to the stronger binding ability between Pi's and Eu3+ than that between TA and Eu3+ [6,15,36], thereby triggering the FL. Thus, the lanthanide ions cause TA-Cu NCs to aggregate, while addition of Pi's causes the lanthanide ion-triggered aggregated TA-Cu NCs to remain dispersed. The degree of redispersion of TA-Cu NCs is directly related to the concentration of Pi's. Hence, this principle could be used for sensing Pi's through the change in FL of the dispersed TA-Cu NCs. To the authors’ knowledge, this study is the first to report on sensing Pi's with small molecule-stabilized Cu NCs as FL probes.

Tannic acid was purchased from Qiangshun Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Na3PO4 and L-ascorbic acid were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemistry Reagent Factory (Sichuan, China). Yb(NO3)3, Tb(NO3)3, and Ce(NO3)3 were from Signopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CuSO4, NaOH, HCl, NaF, and citrate acid were obtained from Chongqing Chemical Reagent Company (Chongqing, China). Eu2O3 (99.99%) was purchased from Signopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used to prepare Eu(NO3)3. Eu(NO3)3 was prepared by dissolving a proper amount of Eu2O3 in 5mL concentrated nitric acid, and then it was recrystallized from the solution containing nitric acid. Finally, Eu(NO3)3 was dissolved in 50mL of ultrapure water. Mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA) was purchased from Aladdin Chem. Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was prepared in the laboratory using a water treatment device. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification, and ultrapure water was used throughout the work. TA-Cu NCs with a diameter of approximately 2.0nm were synthesized as previously reported [35]. In a typical procedure, to prepare fluorescent Cu NCs, 0.2mL CuSO4 (0.1M) aqueous solution was mixed with 0.1mL tannic acid (1mmolL−1) in 20mL distilled water, the resulting mixture was stirred for 5min at room temperature. Then, 0.5mL L-ascorbic acid (0.4molL−1) was added to the mixture and stirred for 6h at 50°C to obtain yellowish Cu NCs. The as-synthesized Cu NCs were stored at 4°C in a refrigerator for further use. The re-prepared Cu NCs was very stable and no obvious change in fluorescence intensity could be found even after two months of storage [35].

The FL spectrum and intensity were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). The FL lifetimes were measured with a time-resolved FL spectrometer model FLS 900 (Edinburgh, England). Zeta potential determination was performed on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). The morphology of the nanoparticles was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, LIBRA 200, ZEISS) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The pH of the solutions was detected by a PHS-3D pH meter (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China).

In a typical process, 30μL of TA-Cu NCs, 600μL Eu(NO3)3, and a limited volume of phosphate ions were mixed in a 10mL volumetric flask. The mixture was diluted to 10mL with ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly. The solution was then incubated for 5min at room temperature. Subsequently, the FL intensity of the mixture was measured by the Type F-7000 FL spectrophotometer with excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 and 430nm, respectively.

A series of working standard solution with different concentrations of Pi's were prepared by diluting a concentrated standard solution of Pi's with water. A blank solution without Pi's was used. The values of [(I−I 0)/I 0] at 430nm versus Pi concentration were used for the calibration, where I 0 is the FL intensity of the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system, and I is the FL intensity of TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system in the presence of standard or sample solution with different concentrations of Pi's. All FL measurements were performed in triplicate, and the data were recorded as a mean.

The applicability of the proposed method for detecting Pi's in a real sample was further evaluated. In this study, wastewater, river water, and lake water samples were investigated. The wastewater sample was taken from the effluent of a wastewater treatment plant located in Beibei (Chongqing, China). River water sample was collected from the Jialingjiang River in the upper reaches of the Three Gorges Reservoir area. Lake water sample was taken from Chongde Lake in the Southwest University. Prior to analysis, the environmental water samples were filtered through 0.45µm filter membrane (Shanghai Xinya Purification Device Factory, Shanghai, China). For Pi determination, 1mL water sample was added into the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ solution (with final TA-Cu NCs concentration of 3μmolL−1 and Eu3+ concentration of 60μmolL−1) and then diluted to 10mL with ultra-pure water. After standing for 5min, the mixture was subjected to FL determination. The standard addition method was adopted to quantify the content of Pi's in the environmental water samples. Determinations were performed by triplicate in three preparations of water samples, and the data were recorded as a mean. A comparison study was performed for Pi analysis according to the molybdenum-blue reference method [37].

The effect of the different lanthanide ions on the FL of the TA-Cu NCs was investigated (Fig. S1A). As shown in Fig. S1A, in the tested concentration range, all tested lanthanide ions, including Eu3+, Yb3+, Tb3+ and Ce3+, caused FL quenching of the TA-Cu NCs. However, the maximum degree of FL quenching of the TA-Cu NCs by Ce3+ was only approximately 14%, which is much lower than that of the other three ions. Furthermore, the recovery extent of FL emission of the TA-Cu NCs-lanthanide ion system after adding Pi's follows the order of Eu3+>Yb3+>Tb3+ (Fig. S1B). This phenomenon is probably due to the higher affinity of Eu3+ to phosphates than that of Yb3+ or Tb3+ [38]. The higher affinity of Eu3+ toward phosphate ions is probably related to the outer electronic structure of Eu3+. The outer electronic structures of Eu3+, Yb3+ and Tb3+ are 4f76S2, 4f146S2 and 4f96S2, respectively. The seven f orbits of Eu3+ are half-filled, leading to its stronger affinity toward phosphate ions. Finally, Eu3+ was selected for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: Eu3+ addition resulted in the evident quenching of the strong blue emission of the Cu NCs solution. The FL spectra indicate that Eu3+ addition decreased the FL intensity of the Cu NCs, but did not affect the FL wavelength ( Fig. 1A). The effects of Eu3+ concentrations on the FL quenching were evaluated (Fig. 1A). The results were analyzed using a classic Stern–Volmer equation: I 0/I=1+K sv[Eu3+], where K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. In the Eu3+ concentration from 1.0μmolL−1 to 60μmolL−1, the fitted equation is I 0/I=1.50×104 [Eu3+]+1.07 (r=0.9857), giving a large K sv of 1.50×104 mol−1 L. The FL lifetime of the TA-Cu NCs was approximately 1.35ns (Table S1). Thus, the quenching rate constant, K q=K sv/τ=1.1×1013 (molL−1 s)−1, indicates an Eu3+-induced effective static quenching of the FL emission [39] of Cu NCs. Time-resolved FL spectra of the TA-Cu NCs and TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ systems were investigated to further explore the FL quenching mechanism. The FL intensity of TA-Cu NCs followed the exponential decay kinetics, and three lifetimes were obtained (Fig. 1B and Table S1). The FL lifetime of the TA-Cu NCs did not change obviously with Eu3+ addition. The diameter of the Cu NCs was around 2nm (Fig. S2A). After Eu3+ addition, large aggregates with a diameter of 3–4nm were clearly observed because of the electrostatic attraction between Cu NCs and Eu3+ (Fig. S2B). The complex formation between Cu NCs and Eu3+ was confirmed by the increased zeta potential and the decreased quenching efficiency with increasing ionic strength [40] after Eu3+ addition (Figs. S3 and S4). These results indicate that Eu3+ acts as a bridge to link neighboring carboxylate and hydroxyl groups on the surface of TA-Cu NCs, thereby quenching the FL of TA-Cu NCs, which lead to TA-Cu NCs aggregate formation. After adding Pi's to the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system, the aggregates formed are redispersed and the diameter decreases to about 2nm (Fig. S2C). Eu3+ was removed from the surfaces of the TA-Cu NCs because Pi's have stronger affinity for Eu3+ than the carboxylate groups on the Cu NCs surface, leading to the recovery of FL emission of Cu NCs ( Fig. 2). The principle of the Cu NCs fluorescent probe for sensing Pi was shown in Scheme 1.

The repeated switching behavior was examined, and good reversible switching of the TA-Cu NCs between the FL turn-off and turn-on states through alternating addition of Eu3+ and Pi's was observed (Fig. S5). A loss in efficiency occurred after 6 full operation cycles under the used conditions. This phenomenon is probably due to the presence of the excess Pi's. To verify this hypothesis, a series of control experiments were performed by varying the Eu3+ concentration at a fixed concentration of Pi's and vice versa. When the concentration of Pi's increased from 60μmolL−1 to 100μmolL−1 at a fixed Eu3+ concentration of 100μmolL−1, the reversible switching behavior of the complexes was degraded (Fig. S5A–C). When the Eu3+ concentration decreased from 120μmolL−1 to 60μmolL−1 at a fixed Pi concentration of 80μmolL−1, the reversible switching behavior of the complexes was also degraded (Fig. S5D–F). The results clearly demonstrate that the reversible switching behavior of the complexes depends on the molar concentration ratio of Pi's to Eu3+. Good reversible switching behavior was observed when the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's reached 1.5. This phenomenon suggests that the dilution effect (caused by the increase in the solution volume with the alternating addition of Eu3+ and Pi) plays a minor role in the degradation of the reversible switching behavior of the copper nanoclusters. When the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's is <1.5, the lower the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's, the higher the extent of the degradation of reversible switching behavior of the copper nanoclusters. For example, the extent of the degradation of reversible switching behavior of the copper nanoclusters increased with a decrease in the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's from 1.25 to 0.75. This condition is due to the fact that a decrease in molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's increases the Pi fraction. Hence, when the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's was <1.5, such as in the current case, the reversible switching of the 3μmolL−1 TA-Cu NCs between the FL turn-off and turn-on states was investigated by alternately adding 100μmolL−1 Eu3+ and 80μmolL−1 Pi's (corresponding to the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's of 1.25). Some Pi's are unspent in the solution because of the first switching of 3μmolL−1 TA-Cu NCs from the FL turn-off state to turn-on state after Pi addition. Thus, in the next cycle, a portion of the added Eu3+ could interact with unspent Pi's in the previous cycle because of the strong interactions between Eu3+ and Pi's, leading to decreased FL quenching of TA-Cu NCs and degradation of reversible switching behavior of the Cu nanoclusters.

In addition, the following parameters were measured: (1) the concentration of Pi's after first addition of Pi's at different molar concentration ratios of Eu3+ to Pi; and (2) the concentration of Pi's at “off” and “on” states corresponding to different measure numbers in the whole full operational cycle process at fixed molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's. The presence of excess Pi's was confirmed from Table S2 and Fig. S6. Furthermore, at “on” state, the concentration of Pi's increased sharply with the increase in measure number when the molar concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's is <1.5 (Fig. S6). These results suggest that the reversibility of the probe may be further improved by controlling suitable concentration ratio of Eu3+ to Pi's.

The kinetic behavior of reactions between the TA-Cu NCs and Eu3+ in the presence of Pi's was evaluated. Eu3+ could decrease the FL intensity of the TA-Cu NCs ( Fig. 3A). The quenching equilibrium was reached in 5min. After adding Pi's, the FL recovery occurred in 5min, showing that the Pi's can rapidly bind Eu3+ ions and displace them from the TA-Cu NCs surfaces. The FL signal remained almost constant with increasing time up to 30min. Thus, 5min reaction time was selected. The FL responses of the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ system were measured by varying the TA-Cu NCs concentration from 1μmolL−1 to 10μmolL−1 (Fig. 3B), Eu3+ concentration from 10μmolL−1 to 180μmolL−1 (Fig. 3C), and pH from 2 to pH 11 (Fig. 3D). The optimal pH, TA-Cu NCs, and Eu3+ concentrations for Pi sensing were 4.0, 3μmolL−1 and 60μmolL−1, respectively.

pH can alter the surface charge state of TA-Cu NCs (Fig. S7). Interestingly, the change in surface charge caused by pH variation did not evidently affect the FL response of the TA-Cu NCs alone, but modulated the FL response of the TA-Cu NCs to Eu3+ (Fig. 3D) with and without Pi's. The FL recovery extent of the TA-Cu NCs-Eu3+ with addition of Pi's reached a maximum at pH 4.0. This condition is probably due to the charge neutralization and the difference in the electrostatic attraction between Cu NCs and Eu3+ at different pH values. To confirm this hypothesis, the zeta potential of the TA-Cu NCs with pH was varied and measured. The zeta potential decreased from positive to zero to negative with increasing pH with the points of zero charge at pH about 3 (Fig. S7). At pH<3, the positively charged surface of the probe caused the repulsion between Cu NCs and Eu3+, leading to incomplete FL quenching. At pH>3, the negatively charged surface of the probe caused the electrostatic attraction between Cu NCs and Eu3+, neutralizing the probe surface charge (Fig. S3). This condition intensifies the FL quenching from pH 3.0 to 5.0. The FL quenching peaked at pH 5.0 by Eu3+. At pH>5.0, the incomplete FL quenching results from the formation of insoluble hydrated oxide [6]. The high FL recovery was observed from pH 3.0 to pH 4.0 compared with other pH levels (Fig. 3D). This condition is probably due to the relatively weak Cu NCs-Eu3+ interactions at more acidic pH. The surface potential of Cu NCs became more negative from pH 3.0 to pH 5.0, making Cu NCs-Eu3+ interactions stronger at pH 5.0 than that at more acidic pH. Thus, the possibility of Eu3+ removal by Pi's from the surface of Cu NCs increases at more acidic pH, leading to high recovery of FL emission of Cu NCs. Finally, pH 4.0 was chosen because of its relative high quenching efficiency and high FL recovery of Cu NCs compared with that at pH 3.0.

The capability of this analytical system for the detection of Pi's was evaluated. The results of (I−I 0)/I 0 were collected and two linear ranges were found (Fig. 2). At low concentration, a linear range was from 0.07μmolL−1 to 1.0μmolL−1 (Fig. 2A, inset), and the regression equation was (I–I 0)/I 0=0.058+0.212 [Pi] (μmolL−1) (r 2=0.9960, n=7). At high concentration, a linear range was from 1.0μmolL−1 to 80μmolL−1 (Fig. 2B, inset), and the regression equation was (I–I 0)/I 0=0.284+0.0134 [Pi] (μmolL−1) (r 2=0.9963, n=8). The LOD for Pi's was 9.6×10−3 μmolL−1 (with an S/N ratio of 3), which was comparable with that obtained by cysteine capped CdS quantum dots and Ag NPs probes [9]. However, it was at least 2-fold lower than those obtained by other FL sensors ( Table 1), showing the high sensitivity of the probe for sensing Pi's.

The response of TA-Cu NCs probes to various environmentally relevant metal ions and anions, including Mn2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, In3+, Na+, K+,
NH 4 +
,
NO 2 -
, F−, Cl−, Br−,
NO 3 -
,
SO 4
2 -
,
CO 3
2 -
, Ac−, SCN−, I−,
CIO 3 -
,
C 2
O 4
2 -
,
SO 3
2 -
,
S 2
O 3
2 -
and urea, were examined. All tested ions, except Fe3+ and Al3+, have no obvious effects on the FL signal even when the concentration of the tested ions in control samples was 10-fold or 100-fold larger than Pi's ( Fig. 4). However, the interferences from Fe3+ and Al3+ can be effectively eliminated by 50μmolL−1 MSA and 100μmolL−1 NaF as masking reagent, respectively. The result indicates the high selectivity of the probe for detecting Pi's over other metal ions and anions.

The applicability of this system for detecting Pi's in a real sample was evaluated. A standard addition method was adopted to detect the concentration of Pi's in environmental water samples from wastewater, Jialingjiang River, and Chongde Lake. The spectra of a wastewater sample using the proposed method is given in Fig. S8. The results of the determination of Pi's well agree with that obtained by the standard spectrophotometric method [37] ( Table 2). The result of t-test performed at the 95% confidence level demonstrates that the two methods did not provide significantly different results (Table 2). The results demonstrate that the proposed Cu NCs FL probes can be used to analyze Pi's in environmental monitoring.

A novel off–on FL probe for sensitive and selective sensing of Pi's was demonstrated. The probe was based on the FL quenching of the TA-Cu NCs by Eu3+ via static quenching mechanism. After adding Pi's, the quenched FL of TA-Cu NCs by Eu3+ recovered because of the higher affinity of Eu3+ ions for the oxygen-donor atoms in Pi's than for the carboxylate groups on the TA-Cu NCs surface. The Cu NCs provided excellent selectivity for Pi's over other metal ions and anions, and also high sensitivity with an LOD of 9.6×10−3 μmolL−1. This study is the first to report an example using Cu NCs as environmentally friendly, low cost, and easily prepared FL probes for sensing Pi's. The low cost of Cu NCs lays a basis for developing a nanoscale sensing platform for diverse analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21277111) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2013D001, XDJK2013A022) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.talanta.2015.05.024.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Standard Method for the Examination of Water and Wastewater Editorial Board, Standard Method for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, fourth ed., Environmental Science Press of China Beijing, China, 2002
